text.title: SLO Error Budget Policy Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules so the error-budget policy is a usable decision artefact, not a doc nobody reads.

rule.statement: The policy MUST define exactly four budget states — healthy (≥50% remaining), yellow (50%→25%), red (25%→0%), exhausted (≤0%) — and assign one concrete action per state.
rule.rationale: The artefact mapping budget state to action (ship / freeze / focus) is missing in current corpus. A four-state ladder is the empirically-converged form (Google SRE, Microsoft, Atlassian) and removes the most common failure: teams paralysed at 12% remaining because no one wrote down what 12% means.

example.code: | State | Budget remaining | Action | |----------|------------------|--------| | Healthy | ≥ 50% | Ship freely, normal cadence | | Yellow | 50% → 25% | Reduce risky changes, prioritise reliability tickets | | Red | 25% → 0% | Release freeze (only customer-facing fixes + reliability) | | Exhausted| ≤ 0% | Escalate to <named-leader>, post-mortem within 5 days |

rule.statement: The policy MUST be signed (name + date) by a product representative AND an engineering representative; un-signed policies are treated as drafts and cannot trigger freezes.
rule.rationale: Teams keep re-litigating freeze vs ship because the policy was written by SRE alone and product never agreed. The dual signature converts the policy into a binding pre-commit, which is the whole reason it works.

rule.statement: Every state MUST name a single decision-maker (handle) authorised to invoke or override the action; "the team decides" is forbidden as decision-maker.
rule.rationale: Plural decision-making at 02:00 → no decision. Naming one human per state means there is always someone to call when the budget is at 24% and a hotfix is queued.

rule.statement: The freeze / yellow states MUST have an explicit exit criterion (e.g. "freeze ends when budget ≥ 30% for 7 consecutive days"); "freeze until next sprint" or undefined exits are rejected.
rule.rationale: Freezes without exit criteria either lift too early (budget re-burns immediately) or persist past usefulness (delivery resentment). A measurable exit aligns engineering and product on a shared signal.

rule.statement: The policy MUST be reviewed every quarter and re-signed; policies with last_reviewed >120 days are flagged stale and lose binding authority until reviewed.
rule.rationale: The policy is decision-grade only as long as the signers still agree. Quarterly review catches the case where the product strategy changed (e.g. trading reliability for speed-to-market) but the policy did not.
